title: Easter egg trees are a forgotten German tradition worth reviving
author: Popkin
summary: Decorating trees with eggs is a springtime tradition that traces back to Germany, where it's known as Ostereierbaum. Families hang brightly painted eggs, often hollowed-out real shells, on branches or small backyard trees. This is a way to celebrate renewal, fertility, and the arrival of warmer weather. —
full_text:
Decorating trees with eggs is a springtime tradition that traces back to Germany, where it's known as Ostereierbaum. Families hang brightly painted eggs, often hollowed-out real shells, on branches or small backyard trees. This is a way to celebrate renewal, fertility, and the arrival of warmer weather. 

German immigrants brought the tradition to other countries, including the United States, where it appeared in the 19th and early 20th centuries. While it never became as widespread as egg hunts or baskets, it looks so much cooler than any Easter tradition I've seen. Over time, it faded from mainstream popularity but never fully disappeared.

In recent years, Easter egg trees have seen a small revival thanks to social media and DIY culture. People are rediscovering the joy of turning trees into colorful, festive displays in the springtime, whether with hand-painted eggs, wooden ornaments, or plastic decorations. Although Easter has passed, I want to start an egg tree in my yard this week because it looks like a whimsical and wonderful activity.
